coredata.dc:title: A simple “turn off-on” ratio fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of dopamine and lysine/arginine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mi, Guohua
Yang, Min
Wang, Cunjin
Zhang, Bin
Hu, Xiaoyun
Hao, Hong
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel and unique “off-on” single-excited dual-emissive ratio fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of dopamine and lysine/arginine has been developed via covalently connecting the yellowish-green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the orange-red fluorescent AgInSe2@ZnS quantum dots (AISe QDs). This ratiometric fluorescence sensor provided with two-emission peaks at 495 and 575 nm under a single-excitation wavelength of 395 nm. The fluorescence of AISe QDs (F575) is effective quenched by dopamine and only efficientlyrecovered by lysine/arginine; meanwhile, the light of CDs (F495) remains unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F575) showed a linear relationship with the concentration of DA in the range of 0–100 μM, and the detection limit as low as 0.21 nM. lysine and arginine with the detection limit of 0.36 nM and 26 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence probe is successfully used to detect DA in human serum. Therefore, the as-synthesized probe shows excellent potential application for the determination of DA in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Dopamine
Lysine
Arginine
CDs
AgInSe2 QDs
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe AC NCs and detection of DA and Lys/Arg
Detection mechanism of DA and Lys/Arg
Selectively detection of DA and Lys/Arg

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image of AC NCs (a) and the lattice fringe of (b).

XRD spectra of AC NCs (a), in which diffraction peak of solid red circle marked and yellow-green cross shape tagged was AISe QDs and CDs, respectively. The (b) was shown the FT-IR spectra of CDs (yellow-green), AISe QDs (red) and AC NCs (orange).
The PL emission of CDs (a), AISe QDs (b) and AC NCs (c). Photostability of AC NCs under UV lamp radiation (365 nm) in 10 h.
The PL lifetime decays of AC NCs and AC NCs-DA.
The impact of incubating time (a) and solution pH (b) on the DA detection.

PL spectra of AC NCs with different concentrations of DA (a). The linear relationship diagram between DA concentration and PL intensity ratio (b), the error bars are stand for standard deviation by three independent measurements.

Fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F575) of AC NCs in the presence of different interfering substance (a): DA (50 μM) and another biological molecule (100 μM). Fluorescence recovery efficiency of AC NCs-DA in the presence of biological molecule (100 μM, b).
The PL spectra of AC NCs-DA with different concentrations of Lys (a). The linear relationship diagram between the concentration of Lys and PL intensity ratio of AC NCs-DA (b).
The PL spectra of AC NCs-DA with different concentrations of Arg (a). The linear relationship diagram between the concentration of Arg and PL intensity ratio of AC NCs-DA (b).
Principle of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for DA/Lys/Arg detection.
Performance comparison of DA sensing method.
Fluorescence lifetimes of AC NCs and AC NCs-DA.
Detection of DA in the human serum sample.

head.ce:title: A simple “turn off-on” ratio fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of dopamine and lysine/arginine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel and unique “off-on” single-excited dual-emissive ratio fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of dopamine and lysine/arginine has been developed via covalently connecting the yellowish-green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the orange-red fluorescent AgInSe2@ZnS quantum dots (AISe QDs). This ratiometric fluorescence sensor provided with two-emission peaks at 495 and 575 nm under a single-excitation wavelength of 395 nm. The fluorescence of AISe QDs (F575) is effective quenched by dopamine and only efficientlyrecovered by lysine/arginine; meanwhile, the light of CDs (F495) remains unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F575) showed a linear relationship with the concentration of DA in the range of 0–100 μM, and the detection limit as low as 0.21 nM. lysine and arginine with the detection limit of 0.36 nM and 26 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence probe is successfully used to detect DA in human serum. Therefore, the as-synthesized probe shows excellent potential application for the determination of DA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a vital neurotransmitter in the brain, which plays a noteworthy role in the central nervous system to regulate a variety of physiological functions and behavioral responses [1,2]. A normal level of DA helps maintain appropriate heart rate, blood pressure, emotion management, and awareness [3,4]. Nonetheless, an excessive amount of DA may lead to fearful neurological disorders such as Huntington’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease and even Schizophrenia [5]. Moreover, other movement disorders are also correlated to the abnormal concentration of DA, such as Ekbom's restless syndrome [6]. Therefore, monitoring DA concentration in serum is of great significance for the clinical diagnostics of correlative psychosis [7,8]. Up to now, there are various methods have been studied (Table 1 ) to detect the concentration of DA in the human's body fluid, such as chemiluminescence [9,10], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], fluorescent method [12,13] and electrochemical methods [14–16]. In recent years, fluorescent methods have attracted more and more attention owing to their extremely fast response, high sensitivity, good selectivity and excellent stability. A variety of fluorescent sensors including small organic molecules [17] and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots shows promising potential in the rapid detection of different substances such as metal ion [18,19], amino acid, organic macromolecule [20], and even tumour cell [21].

By now, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have appealed to enormous interest due to its superb electrical and optical properties, especially CQDs [22–25] and Cd-based [26] or Pb-based [27] QDs. Likewise, QDs-based systems for DA detection have unceasingly been reported. Lan et al. reported the pyrene-derivatized CDs to be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of ferric ions (Fe3+), and with the addition of DA, the strong binding interaction between the catechol groups of DA and Fe3+ can extract Fe3+ from the surface of CDs, and turn on the quenched fluorescence [28]. Wang et al. reported a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with bright blue fluorescence were prepared using glycine and urea by a facile solid-heat method. N-CQDs used as fluorescent probe for the detection of DA based on electron transfer from N-CQDs to dopamine quinone in alkaline condition [29]. Ma et al. proposed a fluorescence strategy for DA detection by element doping graphene quantum dots (GQDs) They systematically discussed the quenching mechanism of DA toward N-doped, S-doped and S, N co-doped GQDs from the surface chemical states and molecule structures [30]. Mani et al. reported MoS2 QDs dispersed over MoS2 nanosheets as the fluorescent probe for the detection of DA based on the foster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [1]. However, most of these reported the detection of DA via the single fluorescent emission signal that possible interferences on the probe surface. The fluorescence intensity possible can fluctuate in the interferences of environmental factors [31]due to they operate on the basis of physical absorbance and direct interaction with the materials [32]. Therefore, the variation repeatability of single fluorescence intensity is not high. The ratio of fluorescence-based detection method is one of the more popular at present that enables quantitative detection of target response based on signal probe, composed of two separate fluorescence emission wavelengths for ratio fluorescent sensor is able to reduce the above-mentioned limitations utilizing self-calibration [33,34], effectively eliminate the background interference, minimize the extrapolation errors [35], environmental factors, significantly improve the sensitivity of detection system [36]. Among them, one emission peak is constant as a reference, while the other one is seriously quenched as a signal response unit.

Lysine (Lys) and arginine (Arg) are essential amino acids of great importance for human health [37]. Lysine has positive nutritional significance in promoting human growth and development, enhancing immunity, relieving anxiety and so on [38,39]. Arginine can promote the conversion of ammonia to urea, thus reducing blood ammonia content. Selective detection of arginine by fluorescent analysis has been widely reported [40–42], but few fluorescent probes for detection of Lys have been reported [43], although Lys has important biological and medical significance.

In this paper, a dual-emitter off-on ratiometric fluorescence sensor consists of AgInSe2@ZnS core@shell QDs and CQDs nanocomposites (AC NCs) have been developed for quantitative detection of DA and subsequent Lys/Arg. This fluorescence sensor provided with two-emission peaks at 495 and 575 nm under a single-excitation wavelength of 395 nm. The yellowish-green fluorescence emission peak of CQDs (CDs) does not change due to insensitiveness to the analyte, while orange-red fluorescence of AgInSe2@ZnS QDs (AISe QDs) is quenched by DA and subsequently recovery by Lys/Arg (Scheme 1 ). The detection limit of the probe can be reached as low as 0.21 nM (DA), 0.36 nM (Lys) and 26 μM (Arg), respectively. Furthermore, the AC NCs sensor displayed a potential application in the field of biomolecular analysis and detection.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), selenium powder (Se), zinc acetate dihydrate (C4H6O4Zn·2H2O), citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine (L-Cys) were acquired from Tianjin Komiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), Thiourea (H2NCSNH2) and glucose (Glu) were supplied from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98%), Indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O), L-glycine (L-Gly), dopaminehydrochloride (DA), amoxicillin (Amo), glutathione (GSH) and uric acid (UA) were offered from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urea was acquired from Tianjin Jindongtianzheng Fine Chemical Factory. Arginine (Arg), L-serine (L-Ser) and folic acid (FA) were acquired Shanghai Lanji Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanol absolute (AR) was acquired from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. L-aspartic acid (L-Asp), L-alanine (L-Ala), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe) and lysine (Lys) were offered from Kehao Bioengineering Co., Ltd. PBS buffer solution was prepared from disodium hydrogen phosphate solution and sodium dihydrogen phosphate solution.

Preparation of AgInSe2@ZnS QDs was according to the reported literature with some modifications [44]. Briefly, 0.0084 g AgNO3, 90 μL MPA were mixed into a three- necked flask containing 100 mL of ultra-pure water and stirred until dissolved by the digital magnetic stirring electric heating sleeve, then 0.117 g InCl3·4H2O was added into the above mixture solution and heated to 60 °C. Besides, the cation precursor solution was obtained by using a NaOH solution (1 M) to adjust the pH of the mixture solution to 8.5. Meanwhile, the transparent Se2−precursor solution was prepared by mixing 0.302 g NaBH4 and 0.3158 g Se powder in 32 mL UP-water and stirring at 50 °C in Ar atmosphere. The 2.8 mL Se2− precursor solution was quickly transferred to the cation precursor solution and heated up to 100 °C for refluxing 1 h. After the reaction temperature cooled down to 40 °C, 10 mL of H2NCSNH2 solution and 10 mL of C4H6O4Zn solution were added into the solution as mentioned above to react at 100 °C for another1 h. Then the resulting yellow-brown solution was AgInSe2@ZnS QDs solution. Moreover, to obtain the purificatory AISe QDs, the absolutely ethanol was used to precipitate and wash the precipitation repeatedly three times by centrifugation, then dried at 60 °C in an oven.

According to the reported literature, CDs were synthesized via solvent-thermal methods [45]. Brief, 0.1 g CA, 0.12 g urea and 40 mL anhydrous ethanol were mixed under vigorous stirring for 10 min, and then the afore-mentioned solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the obtained sample was dialyzed with the 2000D dialysis bag for 24 h. The resulting solution was carbon quantum dots solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe AC NCs and detection of DA and Lys/Arg
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 2 mL AISe QDs solution was added into the colorimetric tube, dropped slowly 80 μL CDs solution with pipette gun and diluted to calibration tail with the pH 7.18 PBS buffer solution. Then the colorimetric tube was placed, and the complex was incubated at room temperature for 30 min. And then, different concentrations of DA solutions were added into the fluorescence sensor system utilizing a standard addition method. Afterwards, the fluorescence intensity of AC NCs probe excited at 395 nm was recorded by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. Subsequently, different concentrations of Lys/Arg were transferred into the above mixture solution. Finally, the fluorescence spectrum was acquired at 395 nm excitation wavelength.

Human blood sample (supplied by schoolinfirmary) was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the serum [2]. And then the supernatant layer was gathered, signed as serum and preserved at 4 °C for further use.

The microstructure of the sensors was investigated by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). The crystal phase of the AC NCs was collected via a Shimadzu XRD 6000 powder diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.1542 Å). The optical properties of the samples were determined by a UV–3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The surfacefunctionalgroups were assayed by Perkin Elmer Frontier. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were measured utilizing F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan).

In order to observe the micro-morphology of AC NCs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of AISe QDs, CDs and AC NCs were carried out respectively. AISe QDs and CDs are uniformly dispersed with the sphere in shape (Fig. S1). It could be seen that AISe QDs appears spherelike (Fig. S1a) and evenly distributed with 2.52–4.45 nm (mean 3.73 nm), the CDs presents spherelike (Fig. S1b) and uniformly disperses with 2.91–10.07 nm (mean 5.67 nm). In Fig. 1 a, it can be observed that the successful covalent binding of AISe QDs and CDs, in which the larger particles are carbon dots and the smaller particles attached to them are AISe QDs. Besides, high-resolution (HR-TEM) from Fig. 1b displays noticeable lattice fringes, in which the graphene network lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.3 nm (yellowish-green mark) are matched with (111) plane of carbon dots with graphene structure, and the lattice stripe of 0.28 nm (red mark) corresponds to (012) plane of Hexagonal AISe QDs, consistent with the XRD pattern.

The XRD spectra were characterized to research the crystallinity and crystal type of AC NCs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, five diffraction peaks could be distinctly observed. Three characteristic diffraction peaks around at 31.8°, 46.2° and 74.6° that might be assigned to the (012), (110) and (016) phases of the AISe QDs, which the (012) phase was the same as the lattice fringe of 0.28 nm in the above HR-TEM image of AC NCs. Another two characteristic peaks at 26.6° and 56.6° corresponded to the (111) and (112) phases of the graphene type CDs (PDF#75-2078). Furthermore, the lattice fringe of 0.30 nm shown in the above HR-TEM image of AC NCs was consistent with the (111) phase of the CDs. The results indicated that the AgInSe2@ZnS QDs-CQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized.

The FT-IR spectra were observed to investigate the distribution of groups on the surface of AC NCs as shown in Fig. 2b. For CDs, the broad absorption band at 3232 cm−1 was assigned to a characteristic NH vibration that the primary amine covalently bound to the CDs surface. The narrow apical absorption band at 1180 cm−1 was ascribed to the CN stretching vibration. The wideband appeared at 3356 cm−1 of AISe QDs was attributed to the OH group, the two symmetric bands at 1392 cm−1 and 1544 cm−1 as a characteristic symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibration of COOH group [46]. For AC NCs, the bands of the strong amide bond at 3201 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of the NH group and CO group [31,47]. The successful synthesizing of the AC NCs was certified by amide bridge formed between carboxyl groups of AISe QDs and amino groups of CDs. The valence states of each element in AISe QDs were investigated by XPS characterization (Fig. S2). The strong signals of C and O are assigned to the MPA ligand. The Ag3d splits into Ag3d5/2 (367.9 eV) and Ag3d3/2 (373.9 eV) peaks, confirming that the valance state of Ag ions is + 1. Furthermore, the valences states of In3+ (In3d5/2 444.9 eV, In3d3/2 452.4 eV), Se2− (Se3d 54.3 and 63.5 eV), Zn2+ (Zn2p3/2 1022.2 eV, Zn2p1/2 1045.2 eV) and S2− (S2p3/2 160.2, 166.1, 175.6 and 179.9 eV) were also confirmed from XPS spectra.

To study of optical performances, the UV–visible spectra and PL spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. S3b, both the AISe QDs and CDs have prominent characteristic absorption peaks and overlap with each other. The fluorescence excitation peaks of CDs at 385 nm and 452 nm, while AISe QDs has a broad fluorescence excitation peaks of 280 nm to 580 nm and maximum value at 405 nm in Fig. S3a. Therefore, the experiment can realize simultaneous excitation of AISe QDs and CDs by selecting an appropriate excitation spectrum. To simultaneously excite the AISe QDs and CDs, we found that 395 nm is the optimal excitation wavelength for the dual-emission fluorescence probe. The fluorescence emission spectrum of AISe QDs, CDs and AC NCs were shown in Fig. 3 a. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs and AISe QDs were 495 nm and 579 nm, which emitted strong yellowish-green and orange-red fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. AC NCs could demonstrate dual-emission bands at 495 nm and 575 nm under single-excitation wavelengths and display orange fluorescence (Fig. 3a inset image).

The stability of AC NCs in aqueous solutions was systematically explored by fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F495/F575) of the AC NCs remains almost unchanged under the ultraviolet lamp (365 nm, 24 W) radiation for 10 h, indicating its prominent photostability (Fig. 3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of DA and Lys/Arg
ce:section.ce:para: Above the dual-emission fluorescence nanocomposites AC NCs, CDs act as the reference signal due to the exciting photostability and striking chemical inertness in the presence of DA (Fig. S4). The orange-red emission AISe QDs play the role of a reaction site for DA that the orange-red fluorescence is effectually quenched by DA and recovered by Lys/Arg. It is probably due to DA readily oxidizing to dopamine-quinone (DA-quinone) under the presence of oxygen [2]; furthermore, the DA-quinone quenched fluorescence of AISe QDs. The quenching principle is that DA-quinone is a powerful electron trapping agent that can accept photogenerated electrons from AISe QDs, change the compound dynamics of the accepted electron and hole of photoexcited AISe QDs [48,49]. This phenomenon is consistent with the reported documentation.

Lys and Arg go for the aliphatic amine and are capable of presenting strong nucleophilic ability which can offer the number of lone pair electrons [37]. As a result, DA-quinone as an electron trapping agent combines preferentially with lone pair electrons of Lys/Arg, the electron-hole recombination rate of AC NCs increased, and the fluorescence gradually recovered.

In order to improve the analytical performance of the dual-emission ratiometric probe, the experimental conditions, including incubating time and solution pH, were optimized. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the degree of fluorescence quenching tends to be stable after 5 min. And therefore, it’s the optimal incubation time for the detection of DA in subsequent experiments.

The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity ratios (F495/F575) of AC NCs and AC NCs-DA were investigated as shown in Fig. 5b. The F495/F575 of AC NCs remains unchanged at faintly acidic pH conditions and gradually increased from 6.81 to 9.18 pH due to significant enhancements in fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. S5). The degree of quenching remains unchanged at faintly acidic pH after the addition of DA, while the F495/F575 of AC NCs-DA shows a trend of gradual increase except for the pH of 7.17 and reaches its maximum at pH 7.17. Therefore, the solution pH of 7.17 is optimized detection condition.

To evaluate the detected sensitivity of AC NCs for DA, the fluorescence response was measured upon adding the different concentration of DA by the standard addition method. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the PL intensity of the AISe QDs (F575) gradually decreases along with the concentration of DA continuously increasing, while the PL intensity of CDs (F495) remains unchanged. That means that the PL intensity ratio (F495/F575) is closed related to the concentration of DA. A linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio (F495/F575) and the concentration of DA was obtained (F495/F575 = 0.149 + 0.0078 × [DA], R2 = 0.997) in the range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 6b). The limit of detection was calculated to be as low as 0.21 nM for DA (S/N = 3, σ wasthree times the background standard deviation). The result implies that the strategy we developed offers a highly sensitive method for detecting DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectively detection of DA and Lys/Arg
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is an essential parameter in the analytical performance of fluorescent sensors. Therefore, the selectivity of the AC NCs-probe for DA was studied under the optimized experimental conditions. The impact of the following probable interfering materials was estimated to ascertain the specificity of DA: L-glycine (L-Gly), L-serine (L-Ser), L-aspartic acid (L-Asp), L-cysteine (L-Cys), glucose (Glu), L-alanine (L-Ala), ascorbic acid (AA), Arginine (Arg), glutathione (GSH), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), lysine (Lys), folic acid (FA), uric acid (UA), urea and norepinephrine (NE). Slight changes of PL intensity ratio were observed upon incubating interfering chemicals, in which DA alone significantly and effectively quenched the fluorescence of the AISe QDs and enhanced PL intensity ratio (Fig. 7 a), indicating that the AC NCs-probe has excellent selectivity to DA.

In order to further explore the selectivity of the probe to DA, possible interfering substances and DA were added to the AC NCs system. Interestingly, Lys and Arg could recover the fluorescence of the AISe QDs (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the detection performance of AC NCs-DA to Lys/Arg was investigated.

The Lys/Arg sensor was established in the AC NCs-DA system. The fluorescence response was investigated to assess the sensitivity of AC NCs-DA for Lys/Arg. The orange-red fluorescence of the DA-quenched AISE QDs was continually recovered with the addition of Lys/Arg, in which the yellowish-green fluorescence of CDs remains almost unchanged (Fig. 8 a and Fig. 9 a). Moreover, a good linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio of AC NCs-DA (F475/F575) and the Lys concentration in the range of 0–20 and 20–140 μM (Fig. 8b). The linear regression equation was F495/F575 = 0.91–0.016 × [Lys] (R2 = 0.99) and F495/F575 = 0.55–0.0014 × [Lys] (R2 = 0.992). The limit of detection was calculated to be as low as 0.36 nM for Lys, indicating that the AC NCs-DA probe has excellent sensitivity to Lys.

Likewise, a linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio of AC NCs-DA (F475/F575) and the Arg concentration in the range from 0.2 to 1 mM (Fig. 9b). The linear regression equation was F495/F575 = 1.137–0.993 × [Arg] (R2 = 0.991), in which the limit of detection was calculated to be as low as 26 μM.

In order to evaluate the practicability of the probe for detecting DA, the experiment of determining the DA concentration in the human serum was carried out by the spiked recovery method because of DA was not detected in the actual human serum. As shown in Table 3 , the recovery is in the range of 96.8% to 102.4%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is from 0.9% to 1.7%, indicating that the sensor has the potential application for detecting DA in real samples.

We have displayed a simple and efficient “turn off-on” ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the high sensitivity and selectivity detection of DA and Lys/Arg. The sensor based on the combination of orange-red fluorescence AISe QDs and yellowish-green fluorescence CDs, in which AISe QDs as the response signal and CD as the reference signal. The fluorescence of AISe QDs is effectively quenched by DA and only efficientlyrecovered by Lys/Arg; meanwhile, the light of carbon dots remains unchanged. Furthermore, the sensor was applied to the detection of DA and Lys/Arg in real human serum and obtained to reliable results. The above consequence indicated that the sensor strategy has a good application prospect in medical diagnosis.

Guohua Mi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Min Yang: Writing - review & editing. Cunjin Wang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Bin Zhang: Software. Xiaoyun Hu: Resources. Hong Hao: Resources, Supervision. Jun Fan: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119555.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MoS2 nanohybrid as a fluorescence sensor for highly selective detection of dopamine
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Pallikkarathodi Mani, M. Ganiga, J. Cyriac, MoS2 nanohybrid as a fluorescence sensor for highly selective detection of dopamine, Analyst, 143 (2018) 1691-1698.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent N, P-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of dopamine and temperature
J. Photochem. Photobiol., B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesizing a nano-composite of BSA-capped Au nanoclusters/graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets as a new fluorescent probe for dopamine detection
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Guo, F. Wu, Y. Ni, S. Kokot, Synthesizing a nano-composite of BSA-capped Au nanoclusters/graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets as a new fluorescent probe for dopamine detection, Analytica Chimica Acta, 942 (2016) 112-120.

J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.K. Pandey, Preeti, K. Rawat, T. Prasad, H.B. Bohidar, Multifunctional, fluorescent DNA-derived carbon dots for biomedical applications: bioimaging, luminescent DNA hydrogels, and dopamine detection, J Mater Chem B, 8 (2020) 1277-1289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A double fluorescent nanoprobe based on phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for detecting dichromate ions and dopamine
RSC Adv.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and convenient fluorescent strategy for the highly sensitive detection of dopamine and ascorbic acid based on graphene quantum dots
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, N. Li, H. Zhang, F. Zhang, X. Su, A simple and convenient fluorescent strategy for the highly sensitive detection of dopamine and ascorbic acid based on graphene quantum dots, Talanta, 189 (2018) 190-195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence detection of dopamine based on carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid
Sens. Actuators, B
